frontmatter.title: Lecture: Earthquake Nucleation and Dynamic Rupture

children.value: This lecture was AI generated with scanned images of hand written notes, but it has not been iterated on. I noticed hallucinations in the graph and remaining challenges in explaining the flow. I will iterate on it.
: Explain why earthquakes nucleate as a frictional instability on faults.
: Describe how elastic stiffness and frictional weakening interact to produce runaway slip.
: Define the critical patch size required for dynamic rupture to begin.
: Explain why some ruptures grow while others stop, using an energy-balance argument.

Kinematic source models prescribe slip as a function of space and time.
Dynamic models solve for slip from the equations of motion plus a friction law —
the slip history is an
Why did slip start in the first place?
To answer this, we need to understand the competition between two processes:
— tectonic forces that increase stress on the fault.
— the fault’s strength that resists slip.
Recall from Module 7d that the energy released during an earthquake partitions as:

What determines whether slip accelerates or stops?

The answer: the competition between the rate at which friction weakens during slip and the rate at which elastic stress drops as the fault relaxes. The simplest model that captures this competition is the

Stability depends on the interplay of two slopes

children.label: Stability depends on the interplay of two slopes

children.alt: Two-panel diagram comparing unstable and stable fault behavior based on the relative slopes of frictional weakening and elastic unloading.

children.value: When friction weakens faster than elastic stress drops (weak spring), slip is unstable — it runs away.
When the spring is stiff enough, elastic unloading outpaces frictional weakening and slip is stable. The instability criterion is

children.value: Instability is not just about friction — it depends on the
elastic stiffness of the loading system
. The same friction law can produce earthquakes or stable creep depending on the fault’s elastic environment.

children.value: means more slip is needed before full weakening — it becomes
an earthquake. The fault is “tougher” and requires a larger initial slipping region to go unstable.

Real faults are not zero-dimensional spring-sliders — they are spatially extended. Earthquakes do not start everywhere on the fault simultaneously. Instead, nucleation proceeds through distinct stages

children.alt: Four-panel diagram showing the stages of earthquake nucleation from a locked fault to dynamic rupture.

children.value: Stages of earthquake nucleation: (1) the fault is locked under tectonic loading; (2) a small patch begins to slip; (3) the slipping region expands to a critical size

children.html: LcL_cLc​

children.value: ; (4) dynamic rupture initiates and propagates rapidly.
The fault is loaded by tectonic motion. Stress builds uniformly (or nearly so).
A localized region begins to slip where stress is highest or friction is lowest. Slip is slow and quasi-static.
The slipping patch expands as it transfers stress to its edges. When it reaches a

children.html: LcL_cLc​

children.value: , the energy released by further growth exceeds the fracture cost.
The patch transitions to rapid, dynamic rupture. Seismic waves are radiated.

LcL_cLc​

children.value: ranges from meters to hundreds of meters.
Earthquakes start locally, within a finite nucleation zone. The entire fault does not fail at once.
Once nucleation succeeds, the rupture front propagates outward. Whether it continues to grow or stops depends on an energy balance at the crack tip

children.alt: Plot of energy release rate increasing with crack length, crossing a horizontal line representing fracture energy, defining the critical nucleation size.

children.value: Small slip patches arrest frequently. Only patches that reach the critical size

children.html: aca_cac​

children.value: can grow into large earthquakes. This is one reason why small earthquakes vastly outnumber large ones.
The homogeneous models above capture the core physics, but real faults are far more complex:
Stress concentrations that can trigger local nucleation
Non-uniform initial conditions; rupture may jump or cascade
Reduce effective normal stress; can promote or inhibit slip
Bends, branches, and step-overs can arrest or redirect rupture
More realistic friction law; velocity- and state-dependent
The spring–slider and slip-weakening models are pedagogical idealizations. Real earthquake nucleation may involve slow slip, foreshocks, cascading micro-failures, or fluid-driven transients. But the
— frictional weakening outpacing elastic unloading — remains the same.
Two faults have the same stress drop

Explain in one sentence why stress drop alone does not predict whether a fault will produce an earthquake.
Using the energy balance framework, explain why small earthquakes are far more common than large ones.

What we deliberately did not cover

children.label: What we deliberately did not cover

children.value: — the more complete friction framework that includes velocity and state dependence
— numerical methods for solving the full elastodynamic problem on heterogeneous faults
— ruptures that exceed the shear wave speed
— feedback between frictional heating, pore fluid pressure, and weakening
The companion notebook (Lab 8) lets you simulate the spring–slider instability, visualize slip-weakening friction for different parameter choices, and explore how the critical nucleation size

children.html: LcL_cLc​

children.value: In the final lecture (Module 9) we step back to see the full architecture of the course — how every module connects — and survey five research frontiers (ML/AI, DAS, earthquake early warning, slow earthquakes, and planetary seismology) that directly extend the concepts developed here.
— comprehensive textbook treatment of earthquake mechanics, friction, and faulting
— the original slip-weakening cohesive zone model for shear cracks
— dynamic rupture solutions with slip-weakening friction
— earthquake nucleation on faults with rate- and state-dependent friction
— comprehensive review of fracture energy and breakdown work (bridges to Module 7d)
